focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

By the time Anika double-knotted her shoes, the old HVAC above Court Two had started its metal cough, three hard rattles and one long whine, like somebody dragging a chair across tin. The sound bounced inside the public rec center and settled in her teeth. She checked Maya's watch out of habit, then corrected herself because it was not a habit yet, not really. She had started wearing it only this week, and the strap still sat wrong on her wrist bone unless she pushed it up with her thumb.

6:43 p.m.

Ravi stood by the cinder-block wall with a clipboard he did not need. He had printed a match sheet from work and clipped it to the board even though the ladder organizer had his own forms. He liked paper that could be held. He liked columns. He liked an object that did not answer back. A smear of bus-depot grease stained the side of his left thumb, and he kept rubbing it with his right index finger as if he could erase it before anyone noticed.

"You warm up through the middle," he said. "Don't chase early winners. Make her hit one more ball every rally. She wants you rushing. Don't give it."

Anika nodded once.

"Run it back."

"Middle first. One more ball."

"And your serve routine."

"Four bounces, breath out, eyes up."

He looked at the watch on her wrist, then at her face, and he did not mention Maya. He never mentioned Maya before matches. It was one of his rules that was not written anywhere.

Across the glass, the ladder board listed names in dry-erase marker. ANIKA IYER sat under RUNG 7. Tonight she played for RUNG 6. Above her name was LEAH SETHI, fourteen, Forest Hills, current holder. Anika knew Leah from two junior events where they had not played each other. Leah had the academy posture that looked taught, shoulders down and neck long, even when s...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ce. Her thumb hovers over not true, then drops. Leaving it there feels like pressing on a bruise.

By the time the rec center comes into view, the sky is already dimming behind the chain-link fence. The building's windows throw back a dull yellow, and she can almost hear the rubber-ball echo before the doors open. She slides the phone into her bag, squares her shoulders, and steps off the bus.

Inside the lobby, she sees Ravi by the vending machine, still in depot uniform, reflective stripes at his cuffs, eyes on the clock. He checks her wet hoodie, then his phone.

"Gate photo says 3:24," he says. "Schedule says 3:22."

"Zoe stopped me at my locker."

"Then you keep walking next time." He turns toward Court 3. "Warm-up is cut. Twelve suicides for the delay, then ladder."

"Dad, the bus--"

"No. You don't lose minutes and then negotiate."

Court 3 smells like wet rubber and old mop water. Two middle-school boys on the next court go quiet when Ravi sets orange cones on the service line and back wall. He clicks his stopwatch and points.

"Touch the line every turn. Hand down so I can see it."

Anika drops her backpack by the glass and starts running. Baseline to short line, backpedal, baseline again. On six her calves knot. On nine she tastes acid and old cafeteria cheese. On eleven her right palm slaps the floor a half second late.

"That one didn't touch," Ravi says. "Again."

She runs it again with her hand flat on the varnish, then one more because she stumbles at the wall and Ravi keeps the watch moving.

When he finally says stop, she braces both hands on her knees and spits thin sour milk into the court-side trash can while kids from the next session file past the glass pretending not to look.

Ravi writes `+14` on the printout clipped to his board. "Now we start."
